content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:6cb6f249

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains durable personal behavior context, a dated prior experience, product interests, and a concrete self-management goal, plus broader rationale around reducing Instagram use during work breaks.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        none

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I / my; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=USER; name=User; reason=First-person statements require the stable namespace-level USER entity; no candidate exists.
- mention=Moment; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=PRODUCT; name=Moment; reason=Concrete reusable app/product discussed repeatedly; no existing candidate.
- mention=Freedom; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<2>; type=PRODUCT; name=Freedom; reason=Concrete reusable app/product discussed; no existing candidate.
- mention=Instagram; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<3>; type=PRODUCT; name=Instagram; reason=Concrete reusable app/product central to the user's goal; no existing candidate.
- mention=social media; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=CONCEPT; name=Social media; reason=Reusable concept needed for claims about reducing usage and prior break.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Moment|Freedom|Instagram|social media
        text_queries=screen time social media usage tracking Moment Freedom|week-long break from social media mid-January|limit Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day Monday to Friday|mindlessly scroll Instagram during breaks at work
        predicate_hints=has_goal|prefers|has_experience_with|metric_observation|current_metric_value

        TARGET_MESSAGE source data:
        [external:provided-text:de53752eedc4f15bc9e1b891e64d3d86] EXTERNAL
Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:6cb6f249:session:1; title=LongMemEval 6cb6f249 session 1; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/03/14 (Tue) 14:30
Transcript:
user: I'm trying to track my screen time and social media usage. Can you suggest some apps or tools that can help me monitor and manage my online activities? By the way, I've been making an effort to cut down on social media lately - I even took a week-long break from it in mid-January, and it was really refreshing.
assistant: Congratulations on taking a week-long break from social media! That's a great step towards digital detox and self-awareness. Here are some apps and tools that can help you track and manage your screen time and social media usage:

**Screen Time Tracking Apps:**

1. **Screen Time** (iOS): A built-in feature on iOS devices that provides detailed reports on app usage, screen time, and notifications.
2. **Digital Wellbeing** (Android): A Google-developed app that tracks screen time, app usage, and provides features like Wind Down mode and App Timer.
3. **Moment** (iOS, Android): Tracks screen time, app usage, and provides features like daily limits, app blocking, and phone-free mode.
4. **Freedom** (iOS, Android): Blocks distracting apps and websites across all your devices, helping you stay focused and reduce screen time.
5. **StayFocused** (Chrome extension): Blocks distracting websites and limits your access to them, helping you stay productive.

**Social Media Tracking and Management Tools:**

1. **RescueTime** (Web, iOS, Android): Tracks how you spend your time on your computer or mobile device, providing insights into your productivity and social media usage.
2. **Hootsuite Insights** (Web): A social media analytics tool that provides insights into your social media usage, including time spent on each platform.
3. **Cold Turkey** (Web, iOS, Android): Blocks social media and other distracting websites, helping you stay focused and avoid mindless scrolling.
4. **SelfControl** (Mac): A free, open-source app that blocks distracting websites and emails for a set period, helping you stay productive.
5. **StayAway** (iOS): Tracks your social media usage and provides features like app blocking, screen time tracking, and goal setting.

**Other Tools:**

1. **Website blockers:** Tools like uBlock Origin, AdBlock, and News Feed Eradicator can help you avoid distracting websites and reduce your social media usage.
2. **Browser extensions:** Extensions like News Feed Eradicator, Facebook Demetricator, and Twitter Demetricator can help you avoid algorithmic feeds and reduce your social media usage.
3. **Phone features:** Many phones have built-in features like Do Not Disturb mode, App Timer, and Screen Time tracking that can help you manage your screen time and social media usage.

Remember, the key to successfully managing your screen time and social media usage is to set goals, track your progress, and be mindful of your behavior. These tools can help you stay on track, but it's up to you to make conscious choices about how you spend your time online. Good luck!
user: I'm interested in trying out some of these apps, especially Moment and Freedom. Can you tell me more about how they work and what kind of features they offer?
assistant: Moment and Freedom are both popular apps that can help you track and manage your screen time and app usage. Here's a more in-depth look at each app:

**Moment:**

Moment is a screen time tracking app that helps you understand how you spend your time on your phone or tablet. Here are some of its key features:

1. **Screen Time Tracking:** Moment tracks how much time you spend on your device, including the number of pickups, screen time, and app usage.
2. **App Tracking:** Moment tracks which apps you use, how often, and for how long. You can even set daily time limits for individual apps.
3. **Goal Setting:** Set daily screen time goals for yourself, and Moment will track your progress and provide reminders to help you stay on track.
4. **Phone-Free Mode:** Moment offers a phone-free mode that locks your device for a set period, helping you take breaks from your phone.
5. **Screen-Free Time:** Set screen-free times, such as during meals or before bed, to help you develop healthier habits.
6. **Family Mode:** Moment offers a family mode that allows you to track and set limits for multiple devices, making it a great tool for parents.
7. **Insights:** Moment provides detailed insights into your app usage, including which apps are most distracting, and when you're most likely to use your phone.
8. **Coach:** Moment's coach feature offers personalized recommendations to help you reduce your screen time and develop healthier habits.

**Freedom:**

Freedom is an app blocker that helps you stay focused by blocking distracting websites and apps across all your devices. Here are some of its key features:

1. **App Blocking:** Freedom blocks distracting apps, such as social media, email, or games, on your phone, tablet, or computer.
2. **Website Blocking:** Freedom blocks distracting websites, such as news sites or YouTube, on your computer or mobile device.
3. **Customizable Blocking:** You can customize which apps and websites are blocked, and when, to fit your needs.
4. **Scheduling:** Schedule blocks in advance to help you stay focused during specific times of the day or week.
5. **Lockdown Mode:** Freedom's lockdown mode blocks all distracting apps and websites, helping you stay focused during intense work sessions.
6. **Syncing:** Freedom syncs your blocked apps and websites across all your devices, so you can stay focused no matter where you are.
7. **Focus Mode:** Freedom's focus mode helps you stay on track by blocking distracting apps and websites, and providing a timer to help you stay focused.
8. **Stats:** Freedom provides statistics on how much time you've saved by blocking distracting apps and websites.

**Pricing:**

Moment offers a free version with limited features, as well as a premium subscription (Moment Premium) that costs $3.99/month or $29.99/year.

Freedom offers a free trial, after which it costs $2.42/month or $29.04/year for individuals. They also offer a family plan that costs $6.99/month or $83.99/year.

Both apps offer a range of features to help you track and manage your screen time and app usage. Moment is more focused on tracking and providing insights, while Freedom is more focused on blocking distracting apps and websites. You may find that using both apps together helps you develop healthier habits and stay focused!
user: I'm particularly interested in Moment's app tracking feature. Can you tell me more about how it tracks apps and how I can use that information to make changes to my phone habits?
assistant: Moment's app tracking feature is one of its most powerful tools for understanding your phone habits. Here's how it works and how you can use the information to make changes:

**How Moment tracks apps:**

Moment tracks which apps you use, how often, and for how long. It does this by monitoring your device's screen time and app usage in the background. When you open an app, Moment records the time you spend in that app, and when you switch to another app or close the app, it logs the time spent. This data is then used to provide insights into your app usage.

**What kind of app tracking data does Moment provide?**

Moment provides a wealth of data about your app usage, including:

1. **App usage time:** The total time you spend in each app, including the number of minutes and hours.
2. **App frequency:** How often you open each app, including the number of times you open it per day.
3. **App duration:** The average time you spend in each app per session.
4. **Time of day:** When you typically use each app, including morning, afternoon, or evening.
5. **Day of the week:** Which days of the week you're most likely to use each app.
6. **Top apps:** A list of your most-used apps, ranked by time spent or frequency.
7. **App categories:** Moment categorizes your apps into groups like social media, games, productivity, and more, helping you understand how you spend your time.

**How to use Moment's app tracking data to make changes:**

Now that you have all this data, how can you use it to make changes to your phone habits? Here are some tips:

1. **Identify time-wasting apps:** Look for apps that take up a disproportionate amount of your time. Consider deleting or limiting your use of these apps.
2. **Set app-specific goals:** Set daily time limits for specific apps, like social media or games, to help you stay within your goals.
3. **Schedule app usage:** Use Moment's scheduling feature to limit your app usage to specific times of the day or week.
4. **Replace bad habits:** Identify apps that are replacing more important activities, like reading or exercise, and make a plan to replace them with healthier habits.
5. **Find alternative apps:** If you find yourself spending too much time in a particular app, look for alternative apps that can help you achieve the same goal in less time.
6. **Set screen-free times:** Use Moment's screen-free mode to set times when you don't want to use your phone, like during meals or before bed.
7. **Reflect and adjust:** Regularly review your app tracking data to reflect on your habits and make adjustments as needed.

By using Moment's app tracking feature, you'll gain a better understanding of your phone habits and be able to make informed decisions about how you spend your time. Remember, the goal is to use your phone in a way that supports your goals and values, not controls your life!
user: I'm really interested in setting some app-specific goals and scheduling app usage. Can you tell me more about how to do that in Moment? For example, how do I set a daily time limit for social media?
assistant: Setting app-specific goals and scheduling app usage are powerful features in Moment that can help you develop healthier phone habits. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to set a daily time limit for social media in Moment:

**Setting a daily time limit for social media in Moment:**

1. **Open Moment:** Launch the Moment app on your device.
2. **Tap on "Apps":** Tap on the "Apps" tab at the bottom of the screen.
3. **Select "Social Media":** Tap on the "Social Media" category or select individual social media apps like Facebook, Instagram, or Twitter.
4. **Tap on "Set Goal":** Tap on the "Set Goal" button next to the social media category or individual app.
5. **Choose a daily time limit:** Select the daily time limit you want to set for social media. You can choose from preset options like 30 minutes, 1 hour, or 2 hours, or set a custom time limit.
6. **Schedule the goal:** Choose the days of the week and the time of day when you want the goal to be active. For example, you might set a goal to limit social media usage to 30 minutes per day, Monday to Friday, from 8am to 10pm.
7. **Save the goal:** Tap "Save" to save your goal.

**How Moment enforces the goal:**

Once you've set the goal, Moment will track your social media usage and alert you when you've reached your daily time limit. Here's how it works:

1. **Tracking:** Moment will track your social media usage in the background, monitoring how much time you spend in social media apps.
2. **Alerts:** When you've reached your daily time limit, Moment will send you a notification reminding you to take a break from social media.
3. **Blocking:** If you've enabled blocking, Moment will temporarily block access to social media apps until the next day or until you've reached a specific time of day when you've allowed yourself to use social media again.

**Tips and variations:**

* **Set multiple goals:** You can set multiple goals for different apps or categories, like setting a separate goal for email or games.
* **Customize your goals:** Experiment with different time limits and schedules to find what works best for you.
* **Use "Free Mode":** If you need to access social media for work or other important reasons, you can enable "Free Mode" to temporarily disable the goal.
* **Review and adjust:** Regularly review your goal progress and adjust your goals as needed to ensure you're meeting your phone habits goals.

By setting app-specific goals and scheduling app usage in Moment, you'll be able to develop healthier phone habits, reduce distractions, and make more intentional choices about how you spend your time.
user: I'm thinking of setting a goal to limit my Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day, Monday to Friday. I've found that I often mindlessly scroll through Instagram during my breaks at work, and I'd like to reduce that. Do you think that's a good goal, and how can I set it up in Moment?
assistant: That's a great goal! Limiting your Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day, Monday to Friday, can help you develop a healthier habit and reduce mindless scrolling. Here's how you can set it up in Moment:

**Setting up the goal in Moment:**

1. **Open Moment:** Launch the Moment app on your device.
2. **Tap on "Apps":** Tap on the "Apps" tab at the bottom of the screen.
3. **Select Instagram:** Tap on the Instagram app icon to open its details page.
4. **Tap on "Set Goal":** Tap on the "Set Goal" button next to the Instagram app icon.
5. **Choose a daily time limit:** Select the daily time limit you want to set for Instagram. In this case, choose 15 minutes.
6. **Schedule the goal:** Choose the days of the week and the time of day when you want the goal to be active. In this case, select Monday to Friday, and choose the time range when you're most likely to take breaks at work (e.g., 9am-5pm).
7. **Save the goal:** Tap "Save" to save your goal.

**Tips to help you achieve your goal:**

1. **Identify your triggers:** Be aware of when and why you usually open Instagram during your breaks. Is it out of boredom, habit, or FOMO? Once you identify your triggers, you can develop strategies to overcome them.
2. **Find alternative breaks:** Replace Instagram with other activities during your breaks, such as taking a short walk, meditating, or reading a book.
3. **Use Moment's coaching:** Moment offers coaching features that can help you stay on track. You can set up reminders, receive motivational messages, or even block Instagram temporarily when you've reached your daily limit.
4. **Review and adjust:** Regularly review your progress in Moment and adjust your goal as needed. If you find it's too difficult to stick to 15 minutes, you can adjust the time limit or schedule.

By setting this goal and using Moment to track your progress, you'll be able to develop a healthier habit and reduce your mindless scrolling on Instagram. Remember, the goal is to be more intentional with your phone usage, not to deprive yourself of social media entirely. Good luck!
user: I think I've got it! I'll set up the goal in Moment to limit my Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day, Monday to Friday, and see how it goes. Thanks for the tips and guidance!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm glad I could help. Setting a goal to limit your Instagram usage is a great step towards developing healthier phone habits. Remember to be patient and kind to yourself as you work on changing your habits. It may take some time to adjust, but with Moment's tracking and coaching features, you'll be able to stay on track and make progress.

Don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further guidance. And don't forget to review your progress regularly to see how you're doing and make adjustments as needed.

Remember, the goal is to find a balance that works for you, not to completely eliminate social media from your life. By being more intentional with your phone usage, you'll be able to free up time and energy for more important things in your life.

Good luck, and I hope you achieve your goal!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.
<env>
Project path: gromozeka-e2e-<id>
Is directory a git repo: No
Platform: Mac OS X
OS Version: 26.5.1
Today's date: <date>
</env>

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:6cb6f249

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: The transcript contains durable personal behavior context, a dated prior experience, product interests, and a concrete self-management goal, plus broader rationale around reducing Instagram use during work breaks.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm trying to track my screen time and social media usage. Can you suggest some apps or tools that can help me monitor and manage my online activities? By the way, I've been making an effort to cut down on social media lately - I even took a week-long break from it in mid-January, and it was really refreshing.
assistant: Congratulations on taking a week-long break from social media! That's a great step towards digital detox and self-awareness. Here are some apps and tools that can help you track and manage your screen time and social media usage:

**Screen Time Tracking Apps:**

1. **Screen Time** (iOS): A built-in feature on iOS devices that provides detailed reports on app usage, screen time, and notifications.
2. **Digital Wellbeing** (Android): A Google-developed app that tracks screen time, app usage, and provides features like Wind Down mode and App Timer.
3. **Moment** (iOS, Android): Tracks screen time, app usage, and provides features like daily limits, app blocking, and phone-free mode.
4. **Freedom** (iOS, Android): Blocks distracting apps and websites across all your devices, helping you stay focused and reduce screen time.
5. **StayFocused** (Chrome extension): Blocks distracting websites and limits your access to them, helping you stay productive.

**Social Media Tracking and Management Tools:**

1. **RescueTime** (Web, iOS, Android): Tracks how you spend your time on your computer or mobile device, providing insights into your productivity and social media usage.
2. **Hootsuite Insights** (Web): A social media analytics tool that provides insights into your social media usage, including time spent on each platform.
3. **Cold Turkey** (Web, iOS, Android): Blocks social media and other distracting websites, helping you stay focused and avoid mindless scrolling.
4. **SelfControl** (Mac): A free, open-source app that blocks distracting websites and emails for a set period, helping you stay productive.
5. **StayAway** (iOS): Tracks your social media usage and provides features like app blocking, screen time tracking, and goal setting.

**Other Tools:**

1. **Website blockers:** Tools like uBlock Origin, AdBlock, and News Feed Eradicator can help you avoid distracting websites and reduce your social media usage.
2. **Browser extensions:** Extensions like News Feed Eradicator, Facebook Demetricator, and Twitter Demetricator can help you avoid algorithmic feeds and reduce your social media usage.
3. **Phone features:** Many phones have built-in features like Do Not Disturb mode, App Timer, and Screen Time tracking that can help you manage your screen time and social media usage.

Remember, the key to successfully managing your screen time and social media usage is to set goals, track your progress, and be mindful of your behavior. These tools can help you stay on track, but it's up to you to make conscious choices about how you spend your time online. Good luck!
user: I'm interested in trying out some of these apps, especially Moment and Freedom. Can you tell me more about how they work and what kind of features they offer?
assistant: Moment and Freedom are both popular apps that can help you track and manage your screen time and app usage. Here's a more in-depth look at each app:

**Moment:**

Moment is a screen time tracking app that helps you understand how you spend your time on your phone or tablet. Here are some of its key features:

1. **Screen Time Tracking:** Moment tracks how much time you spend on your device, including the number of pickups, screen time, and app usage.
2. **App Tracking:** Moment tracks which apps you use, how often, and for how long. You can even set daily time limits for individual apps.
3. **Goal Setting:** Set daily screen time goals for yourself, and Moment will track your progress and provide reminders to help you stay on track.
4. **Phone-Free Mode:** Moment offers a phone-free mode that locks your device for a set period, helping you take breaks from your phone.
5. **Screen-Free Time:** Set screen-free times, such as during meals or before bed, to help you develop healthier habits.
6. **Family Mode:** Moment offers a family mode that allows you to track and set limits for multiple devices, making it a great tool for parents.
7. **Insights:** Moment provides detailed insights into your app usage, including which apps are most distracting, and when you're most likely to use your phone.
8. **Coach:** Moment's coach feature offers personalized recommendations to help you reduce your screen time and develop healthier habits.

**Freedom:**

Freedom is an app blocker that helps you stay focused by blocking distracting websites and apps across all your devices. Here are some of its key features:

1. **App Blocking:** Freedom blocks distracting apps, such as social media, email, or games, on your phone, tablet, or computer.
2. **Website Blocking:** Freedom blocks distracting websites, such as news sites or YouTube, on your computer or mobile device.
3. **Customizable Blocking:** You can customize which apps and websites are blocked, and when, to fit your needs.
4. **Scheduling:** Schedule blocks in advance to help you stay focused during specific times of the day or week.
5. **Lockdown Mode:** Freedom's lockdown mode blocks all distracting apps and websites, helping you stay focused during intense work sessions.
6. **Syncing:** Freedom syncs your blocked apps and websites across all your devices, so you can stay focused no matter where you are.
7. **Focus Mode:** Freedom's focus mode helps you stay on track by blocking distracting apps and websites, and providing a timer to help you stay focused.
8. **Stats:** Freedom provides statistics on how much time you've saved by blocking distracting apps and websites.

**Pricing:**

Moment offers a free version with limited features, as well as a premium subscription (Moment Premium) that costs $3.99/month or $29.99/year.

Freedom offers a free trial, after which it costs $2.42/month or $29.04/year for individuals. They also offer a family plan that costs $6.99/month or $83.99/year.

Both apps offer a range of features to help you track and manage your screen time and app usage. Moment is more focused on tracking and providing insights, while Freedom is more focused on blocking distracting apps and websites. You may find that using both apps together helps you develop healthier habits and stay focused!
user: I'm particularly interested in Moment's app tracking feature. Can you tell me more about how it tracks apps and how I can use that information to make changes to my phone habits?
assistant: Moment's app tracking feature is one of its most powerful tools for understanding your phone habits. Here's how it works and how you can use the information to make changes:

**How Moment tracks apps:**

Moment tracks which apps you use, how often, and for how long. It does this by monitoring your device's screen time and app usage in the background. When you open an app, Moment records the time you spend in that app, and when you switch to another app or close the app, it logs the time spent. This data is then used to provide insights into your app usage.

**What kind of app tracking data does Moment provide?**

Moment provides a wealth of data about your app usage, including:

1. **App usage time:** The total time you spend in each app, including the number of minutes and hours.
2. **App frequency:** How often you open each app, including the number of times you open it per day.
3. **App duration:** The average time you spend in each app per session.
4. **Time of day:** When you typically use each app, including morning, afternoon, or evening.
5. **Day of the week:** Which days of the week you're most likely to use each app.
6. **Top apps:** A list of your most-used apps, ranked by time spent or frequency.
7. **App categories:** Moment categorizes your apps into groups like social media, games, productivity, and more, helping you understand how you spend your time.

**How to use Moment's app tracking data to make changes:**

Now that you have all this data, how can you use it to make changes to your phone habits? Here are some tips:

1. **Identify time-wasting apps:** Look for apps that take up a disproportionate amount of your time. Consider deleting or limiting your use of these apps.
2. **Set app-specific goals:** Set daily time limits for specific apps, like social media or games, to help you stay within your goals.
3. **Schedule app usage:** Use Moment's scheduling feature to limit your app usage to specific times of the day or week.
4. **Replace bad habits:** Identify apps that are replacing more important activities, like reading or exercise, and make a plan to replace them with healthier habits.
5. **Find alternative apps:** If you find yourself spending too much time in a particular app, look for alternative apps that can help you achieve the same goal in less time.
6. **Set screen-free times:** Use Moment's screen-free mode to set times when you don't want to use your phone, like during meals or before bed.
7. **Reflect and adjust:** Regularly review your app tracking data to reflect on your habits and make adjustments as needed.

By using Moment's app tracking feature, you'll gain a better understanding of your phone habits and be able to make informed decisions about how you spend your time. Remember, the goal is to use your phone in a way that supports your goals and values, not controls your life!
user: I'm really interested in setting some app-specific goals and scheduling app usage. Can you tell me more about how to do that in Moment? For example, how do I set a daily time limit for social media?
assistant: Setting app-specific goals and scheduling app usage are powerful features in Moment that can help you develop healthier phone habits. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to set a daily time limit for social media in Moment:

**Setting a daily time limit for social media in Moment:**

1. **Open Moment:** Launch the Moment app on your device.
2. **Tap on "Apps":** Tap on the "Apps" tab at the bottom of the screen.
3. **Select "Social Media":** Tap on the "Social Media" category or select individual social media apps like Facebook, Instagram, or Twitter.
4. **Tap on "Set Goal":** Tap on the "Set Goal" button next to the social media category or individual app.
5. **Choose a daily time limit:** Select the daily time limit you want to set for social media. You can choose from preset options like 30 minutes, 1 hour, or 2 hours, or set a custom time limit.
6. **Schedule the goal:** Choose the days of the week and the time of day when you want the goal to be active. For example, you might set a goal to limit social media usage to 30 minutes per day, Monday to Friday, from 8am to 10pm.
7. **Save the goal:** Tap "Save" to save your goal.

**How Moment enforces the goal:**

Once you've set the goal, Moment will track your social media usage and alert you when you've reached your daily time limit. Here's how it works:

1. **Tracking:** Moment will track your social media usage in the background, monitoring how much time you spend in social media apps.
2. **Alerts:** When you've reached your daily time limit, Moment will send you a notification reminding you to take a break from social media.
3. **Blocking:** If you've enabled blocking, Moment will temporarily block access to social media apps until the next day or until you've reached a specific time of day when you've allowed yourself to use social media again.

**Tips and variations:**

* **Set multiple goals:** You can set multiple goals for different apps or categories, like setting a separate goal for email or games.
* **Customize your goals:** Experiment with different time limits and schedules to find what works best for you.
* **Use "Free Mode":** If you need to access social media for work or other important reasons, you can enable "Free Mode" to temporarily disable the goal.
* **Review and adjust:** Regularly review your goal progress and adjust your goals as needed to ensure you're meeting your phone habits goals.

By setting app-specific goals and scheduling app usage in Moment, you'll be able to develop healthier phone habits, reduce distractions, and make more intentional choices about how you spend your time.
user: I'm thinking of setting a goal to limit my Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day, Monday to Friday. I've found that I often mindlessly scroll through Instagram during my breaks at work, and I'd like to reduce that. Do you think that's a good goal, and how can I set it up in Moment?
assistant: That's a great goal! Limiting your Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day, Monday to Friday, can help you develop a healthier habit and reduce mindless scrolling. Here's how you can set it up in Moment:

**Setting up the goal in Moment:**

1. **Open Moment:** Launch the Moment app on your device.
2. **Tap on "Apps":** Tap on the "Apps" tab at the bottom of the screen.
3. **Select Instagram:** Tap on the Instagram app icon to open its details page.
4. **Tap on "Set Goal":** Tap on the "Set Goal" button next to the Instagram app icon.
5. **Choose a daily time limit:** Select the daily time limit you want to set for Instagram. In this case, choose 15 minutes.
6. **Schedule the goal:** Choose the days of the week and the time of day when you want the goal to be active. In this case, select Monday to Friday, and choose the time range when you're most likely to take breaks at work (e.g., 9am-5pm).
7. **Save the goal:** Tap "Save" to save your goal.

**Tips to help you achieve your goal:**

1. **Identify your triggers:** Be aware of when and why you usually open Instagram during your breaks. Is it out of boredom, habit, or FOMO? Once you identify your triggers, you can develop strategies to overcome them.
2. **Find alternative breaks:** Replace Instagram with other activities during your breaks, such as taking a short walk, meditating, or reading a book.
3. **Use Moment's coaching:** Moment offers coaching features that can help you stay on track. You can set up reminders, receive motivational messages, or even block Instagram temporarily when you've reached your daily limit.
4. **Review and adjust:** Regularly review your progress in Moment and adjust your goal as needed. If you find it's too difficult to stick to 15 minutes, you can adjust the time limit or schedule.

By setting this goal and using Moment to track your progress, you'll be able to develop a healthier habit and reduce your mindless scrolling on Instagram. Remember, the goal is to be more intentional with your phone usage, not to deprive yourself of social media entirely. Good luck!
user: I think I've got it! I'll set up the goal in Moment to limit my Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day, Monday to Friday, and see how it goes. Thanks for the tips and guidance!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm glad I could help. Setting a goal to limit your Instagram usage is a great step towards developing healthier phone habits. Remember to be patient and kind to yourself as you work on changing your habits. It may take some time to adjust, but with Moment's tracking and coaching features, you'll be able to stay on track and make progress.

Don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further guidance. And don't forget to review your progress regularly to see how you're doing and make adjustments as needed.

Remember, the goal is to find a balance that works for you, not to completely eliminate social media from your life. By being more intentional with your phone usage, you'll be able to free up time and energy for more important things in your life.

Good luck, and I hope you achieve your goal!

responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor
responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.

structured.fullText: {"notes":[{"candidate_claim_hints":["limit my Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day, Monday to Friday","mindlessly scroll through Instagram during my breaks at work"],"confidence":0.98,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<1>","role":"secondary"},{"entity_id":"entity:<3>","role":"primary"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I'll set up the goal in Moment to limit my Instagram usage to 15 minutes a day, Monday to Friday, and see how it goes.","evidence_reason":"The quote states the concrete intended setup and evaluation plan for reducing Instagram usage with Moment.","importance":4,"keywords":["Instagram","Moment","15 minutes a day","Monday to Friday","work breaks","mindless scrolling","screen time"],"note_type":"plan","rationale":"This is more than an atomic fact: it captures a self-management plan with motivation, tool choice, target limit, schedule, and intended trial/evaluation.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's personal digital-wellbeing plan for reducing Instagram use during work breaks"},"summary":"On 2023-03-14, the user planned to use Moment to limit Instagram usage to 15 minutes per day, Monday through Friday, because they often mindlessly scroll Instagram during work breaks and want to reduce that habit. The intended approach was to set an app-specific Moment goal and evaluate how it goes.","tags":["digital_wellbeing","screen_time","habit_change","social_media"],"title":"Instagram work-break reduction plan using Moment","valid_from":"2023-03-14T14:30:00+02:00","valid_to":null},{"candidate_claim_hints":["took a week-long break from social media in mid-January","was really refreshing"],"confidence":0.95,"entity_refs":[{"entity_id":"entity:<0>","role":"owner"},{"entity_id":"entity:<4>","role":"primary"}],"evidence_kind":"direct","evidence_quote":"I even took a week-long break from it in mid-January, and it was really refreshing.","evidence_reason":"The quote directly describes the prior social media break and the user's positive assessment of it.","importance":3,"keywords":["social media break","mid-January","refreshing","digital detox","cut down"],"note_type":"lesson","rationale":"This is useful as a reusable lesson about what has worked for the user, not just a single clean attribute; it connects an experience with its perceived effect.","scope_json":{"basis":"summarized","kind":"entity","text":"User's prior experience with reducing social media usage"},"summary":"Before 2023-03-14, the user had been trying to cut down on social media and reported that a week-long break from social media in mid-January felt refreshing, suggesting that temporary abstinence has been a positive strategy for them.","tags":["digital_wellbeing","prior_experience","social_media"],"title":"Prior social media break felt refreshing","valid_from":"2023-03-14T14:30:00+02:00","valid_to":null}]}
